segments.s597-g105: ببستند is the 3rd-person plural past of بستن used here in the sense 'fastened/tied'. The phrase بر پایه تخت عاج is a prepositional phrase with بر + a noun group 'پایه تخت عاج' meaning 'the pedestal/base of the ivory throne' (پایه = pedestal, تخت = throne, عاج = ivory). Word order is flexible/classical: verb (ببستند) precedes the locative phrase.
segments.s597-g106: نهادند is the 3rd-person plural past of نهادن 'to place/set' meaning 'they placed'. The locative/goal is expressed by بر گوشه عاج (بر + گوشه عاج = 'on a corner/edge of the ivory'), and تاج is the item placed (the crown). Classical word order places the prepositional phrase after the verb; the noun تاج is the direct object placed at that location.
segments.s597-g107: This is a compact verbless/clausal unit: جهانی (the whole world) is the agent and نظاره (a noun 'gaze/view') names the action; بران is a contracted classical form of بر آن ('upon that') linking the gaze to the following objects تاج و تخت ('crown and throne'). The phrase thus means 'the whole world gazed upon that crown and throne.'
segments.s597-g108: که تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result ('so that' / 'to see whether'). چون here is the interrogative/adverb 'how', بود is past 'was/were' (classical narrative past used for reported/future-in-reference), and کار آن نیک‌بخت means 'the affairs of that fortunate one' (کار = affairs; آن modifies نیک‌بخت). Together: 'to see how the affairs of that fortunate one were.'
segments.s597-g109: که‌گر (که گر) is an archaic conditional 'if'. شاه پیروز گردد: شاه (king) + پیروز (victorious) + گردد, here a present/subjunctive/passive-form of گشت/گردیدن used to express 'becomes' or 'should become' (a classical subjunctive after the conditional). برین is the fused form بر این ('on/over this' / 'in this matter'), adverbially referring to the situation at hand.
segments.s597-g110: برو here functions as a narrative/transition particle ('thereupon/then') in classical usage rather than a literal imperative. شهریاران is the plural subject 'the nobles/lords', کنند is 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive of کردن used as a light verb 'will do/utter', and آفرین is the acclamation/praise they utter. Together: 'thereupon the nobles will cry
segments.s597-g9: چه is the interrogative 'what'; پیش here means 'forward' or 'before' and is placed before the verb for emphasis. آری is an archaic/poetic verb form meaning 'you bring' (compare modern آوری); از داد و از راستی are two prepositional phrases 'from justice and from righteousness' that serve as the source/medium of whatever is being brought. The whole segment asks what one can produce or present through justice and truth.
segments.s597-g10: کزان ('from which') refers back to 'از داد و از راستی'; گم is an adjective 'lost' and شود is the verb 'become' (here simple/present sense 'be lost'). The verb precedes its objects in this poetic line, so کژی و کاستی ('crookedness and deficiency') are the things that 'become lost' as a result.
segments.s597-g11: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the verb; داد is the verb 'gave' (not the noun 'justice' here), and پاسخ is its direct object 'an answer'. به فرزانگان is the indirect object 'to the sages/learned men'. The verb-object order (داد پاسخ) is a common poetic variant of the normal SOV.
segments.s597-g12: بدان is here the prepositional phrase 'to those' (split in the word list as ب + دان; دان in this line functions as part of that demonstrative/locative), governing the following coordinated nouns. نامداران و مردانگان are the two groups being addressed: 'the renowned' and 'the men/heroes'.
segments.s597-g13: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / purpose or content). بخشش is 'giving/donation' (generosity) and بیفزایم is 1st person optative/subjunctive 'may I increase' or 'I will add/increase' (بی‑ prefix plus افزایم). از گفت‌وگوی begins a prepositional phrase 'from/away from disputation/conversation' that connects to the next segment.
segments.s597-g14: بکاهم is a 1st person verb form meaning roughly 'I will refrain/lessen' (an archaic/poetic form; the exact lemma is irregular in the provided list). ز is the poetic form of از 'from'; بیدادی is the noun 'injustice/cruelty'; جست و جوی is a compound 'seeking/search' (جست here = seek, جوی = search). The sequence lists the things the speaker will avoid: disputation, injustice, and futile seeking.
segments.s597-g15: کسی 'someone/anyone' is marked by the object/topic marker را; کجا 'where' introduces the interrogative/existential question; پادشاهی is 'kingship' and سزاست is the impersonal/present verb 'is deserved' (it is fitting). Together the clause asks rhetorically where one can be found who deserves to be king.
segments.s597-g16: زمین 'land' is the direct object (marked by را); بدیشان 'to them' (poetic form of به + ایشان) indicates the recipients; ببخشیم is the 1st person plural subjunctive/optative 'we shall bestow/give' (here 'I/we will grant'); راست is an adverb 'justly/rightly' qualifying how the land will be given. The clause promises to give the land rightly to those people.
segments.s597-g33: ز (preposition) + دستور (object) + پرسیم (1st-person plural present/subjunctive) forms an expression 'we ask from/seek instruction'. یکسر سخن ('one-straight speech') follows as an adverbial/accusative phrase: یکسر (entirely/straightforwardly) modifies سخن (speech). The verb پرسیم governs the whole phrase and sets the speech-act: 'we ask (for) instruction and (we) speak straight.'
segments.s597-g34: چو (temporal/conditional conjunction 'when'/'if') introduces a subordinate clause. کاری نو افگند is a compact poetic clause: کاری نو = 'a new deed/undertaking' and افگند is an archaic past/preterite root used here in a generalizing sense 'undertakes/sets up' (poetic diction). خواهم is 1st-person singular/future auxiliary 'I will', and ز بن is a prepositional phrase 'from the root' (i.e., utterly, to the root). Together: 'when someone sets up a new thing, I will (put it down) from the root.'
segments.s597-g35: کسی کو همی داد خواهد ز من is a relative nominal phrase: کسی 'someone', کو (archaic relative/cohesive particle) links to the following clause, همی gives a continuous/emphatic tone, and داد خواهد ز من means 'wants/asks justice from me' (خواهد is 3rd-person-future/intent 'he will/wants'). The whole phrase = 'who asks justice of me / who wants justice from me.'
segments.s597-g36: نجویم is first-person singular with prohibitive/negative prefix ن- (I will not seek); the lemma shows 'seek' as the core meaning. پراگندن 'to scatter/spread' is a verbal complement, and انجمن 'assembly/meeting' is its object. Word order is verb (negated) + infinitival/masdar-like complement + object: 'I will not seek to scatter the assembly.'
segments.s597-g37: دهم (1st-person present/future-like 'I give') takes داد ('justice') as its object: 'I give justice'. آنکس که او داد خواست is a relative clause: آنکس 'that person' + که (who/that) + او داد خواست 'he asked/wanted justice' (خواست is past tense 'asked/wanted'). Entire segment = 'I give justice to the one who asked for it.'
segments.s597-g38: به چیزی نرانم is a negative first-person construction: نرانم (from راندن, 'I do not drive/turn') with به چیزی 'toward anything' marking the target; سخن ('speech') is the theme, and جز به راست ('except toward the right/true') is a restrictive clause. Syntax: 'I will not turn my speech to anything except the truth.' The preposition به recurs to mark directional/target sense.
segments.s597-g39: مکافات سازم is a nominative/object + verb: مکافات 'retribution/punishment' is the thing being made/inflicted and سازم (1st-person present/subjunctive) 'I will make/exact'. بدان را is an archaic compound meaning 'upon him/that one' (به + آن = بدان) with را as definite object marker; به بد ('with evil') gives the manner. Overall: 'I will exact punishment upon him with evil.'
segments.s597-g40: چنان کز ره شهریاران سزد is a final clause giving manner/reason: چنان 'so/in such a way', کز = 'that/from which' introduces the standard, ره شهریاران 'the way/course of kings' (ره = way; شهریاران = kings/lords), and سزد is an impersonal/present-subjunctive verb 'it befits' or 'is fitting'. Together: 'in such a way that it is fitting by the ways of kings.'
segments.s597-g57: ز داد is a prepositional phrase 'from/of Justice' (ز = 'from'); آفریدست is an archaic/poetic 3rd‑person past form meaning 'created / made'; ایزد is the subject 'God'; ورا is a clitic object/topic 'him' (ʻto him/for himʼ). Word order is poetic (prepositional phrase + verb + subject + object) and yields the sense 'God created him from Justice.'
segments.s597-g58: مبادا که is a negative jussive formula 'may it not be that / lest'; کاری here means 'some action/thing' (lexical senses supplied don't match exactly, so sense is marked unknown below); رسد is the verb 'to happen / befall' (present/subjunctive sense used in the subordinate clause after مبادا); بد ورا = 'something bad to him' (بد = 'bad', ورا = 'to him'). The clause as a whole means 'may no bad thing befall him.'
segments.s597-g59: به گفتار is an adverbial 'in/with words' (به + گفتار); اگر introduces a condition 'if'; هیچ here means 'at all/ever' (emphatic limiter); تاب آوریم is a verbal phrase 'we bear/endure' (تاب = 'endurance', آوریم = 1st‑pl present/subjunctive of آوردن used here in the idiom تاب آوردن 'to endure'). The whole phrase = 'if we can endure at all by speech.'
segments.s597-g60: خرد را: خرد 'wisdom' marked by را as definite/direct object/topic; همی is the archaic present/emphatic particle 'still/indeed' giving continuous or emphatic force; سر به خواب آوریم is literal 'bring the head to sleep' (سر = 'head', به خواب آوردن = 'to put to sleep'), meaning 'we lull wisdom (into) sleep' or 'we put reason to sleep.' آوریم is again 1st‑pl present form of آوردن 'we bring/cause.'
segments.s597-g61: همه نیکویها is the object phrase 'all good things' (همه = 'all', نیکویها = plural 'good things/virtues'); بیابیم is 1st‑pl present/subjunctive/optative of یابیدن 'we find / may we find' (poetic mode), and ازوی 'from him' marks source. Together: 'we find all good things from him.'
segments.s597-g62: به خورد و به داد are two prepositional phrases 'with/for food and with/for justice' (به = 'to/for', خورد here functioning nominally as 'food/sustenance' and داد = 'justice'); اندر آریم ('اندر' = 'in/among', آریم = 1st‑pl present/subjunctive of آوردن 'we place / we bring') and روی = 'upon the face'. The clause means 'we place upon his face (i.e. bestow) food and justice.'
segments.s597-g63: بدین here is the archaic adverb 'by/with this' (poetic contraction of ب + این); برز بالا = 'exalted height / lofty position' (برز = 'high/exalted', بالا = 'high'); و این شاخ و یال = 'and this horn and mane' (شاخ = 'horn', یال = 'mane'). This enumerates his high status and physical glory in poetic parallelism.
segments.s597-g64: به گیتی 'in the world' (به = locative particle, گیتی = 'world'); کسی نیست = 'there is no one' (کسی used generically 'anyone/no one', نیست = 'is not'); او را همال = 'equal to him' with او را as emphatic/contrastive object and همال meaning 'peer/equal' (figurative). The clause states 'there is no one in the world equal to him.'
segments.s597-g49: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning "when"; پاسخ is the direct object "reply/answer" placed before the verb; شنیدند is the 3rd‑person plural past tense "they heard." آن بخردان is a demonstrative + plural noun phrase "those wise ones" that supplies the subject (they).
segments.s597-g50: This is an appositive noun phrase further identifying the referents: بزرگان "the great ones" joined by و and then a compound modifier بیداردل موبدان "wakeful‑hearted mōbeds/priestly figures." موبدان is the plural of موبد (mōbed).
segments.s597-g51: ز here introduces the cause "because of/from" what follows; گفت is a noun meaning "speech/words" and گذشته modifies it as "past/previous (words)." پشیمان شدند is the verbal predicate "they became regretful" (past, 3rd plural).
segments.s597-g52: گنه کارگان is a compound meaning "those who have sinned / the guilty (doers)"; سوی is the preposition "toward(s)" and درمان is the noun "remedy/cure." شدند again is the 3rd‑person plural past "they became/turned (toward)." The clause means the sinners turned toward seeking a cure/remedy.
segments.s597-g53: به + آواز forms an adverbial phrase "by/with a voice" = "aloud." گفتند is the past plural "they said" and یک با دگر is an idiomatic expression "one with another" = "to one another." Together: "they said aloud to each other."
segments.s597-g54: که introduces a content clause "that..." شاهی here means "kingship/throne." بود is the past copula "was." زین is a poetic noun literally "saddle/seat" used metonymically for the throne, and سزاوارتر is the comparative "more deserving/fit." The clause: "that he was more deserving of the throne."
segments.s597-g55: به introduces a frame of comparison/assessment "in/regarding." The following nouns form a linked list of qualities: مردی (here meaning "manliness/courage"), گفتار "speech," رای "counsel/decision," and نژاد "lineage/descent." The phrase enumerates the domains in which the person excels.
segments.s597-g56: ازین is the comparative marker "than this/than him" (زِ + این in poetry) introduced by از; پاک‌تر is the comparative "purer." در جهان "in the world" is the locative phrase; کس نزاد is a compact predicative unit meaning "(no) one was born" — together: "no one in the world was born purer (than him)."
segments.s597-g73: Нدانست is a past negative form meaning 'knew not' (here functioning as 'no one knew'); the indefinite subject کس follows the verb in inverted order and here means 'no one'. در هنرهای تو is a locative/field phrase ('in/among your arts/skills') that specifies the domain about which nothing was known.
segments.s597-g74: به introduces the following noun phrase in the sense 'concerning' or 'with respect to' (here translated 'of' / 'about'); پاکی تن is a genitive-like phrase 'purity of the body' with تن = 'body', and the subsequent nouns دانش و رای are coordinated by و and share the same governing relation to به; the final تو is the possessive 'your' applying to all three nouns.
segments.s597-g75: چو introduces a temporal clause ('when'); the relative clause که بود از نژاد پشین ('who was of an earlier lineage') modifies خسرو. The verb بود is past tense 'was' and the prepositional phrase از نژاد پشین gives origin/lineage ('from an earlier stock').
segments.s597-g76: خواندند is past plural 'they called/cried' (here reporting the crowd's acclamation). به شاهی + برو functions as the idea of 'to kingship / to the throne' with برو used as an exhortatory/infinitival notion ('go to kingship' or 'to the throne'); آفرین is the exclamation 'Afarin' (bravo) that they uttered. In classical Persian reported exclamations often appear without quotation marks.
segments.s597-g77: همه is the subject 'all / everyone'. زیر سوگند و بند is a prepositional phrase 'under oath and bond' (زیر = 'under', سوگند = 'oath', بند = 'bond/fetter'). وییم is the archaic/persianized present plural copula 'we are'.
segments.s597-g78: The first که introduces a subordinate clause; گوید is a present-tense reporting verb 'he says' used here to introduce reported content, followed by another که ('that') introducing the content clause اندر گزند وییم — 'we are in harm/danger'. اندر = 'in/within' and گزند = 'harm/damage'; وییم again is 'we are'. (Note: the lexical senses provided for گوید do not include the verb 'say', so the appropriate sense index is not available.)
segments.s597-g79: گرو is a noun 'a band / troop' (here 'a retinue' or 'band of followers'). زین سپس is an archaic adverbial phrase meaning 'from this / thereafter' (زین = 'from this' in classical usage, followed by سپس = 'then/afterwards'), and شاه ایران بود is 'the Shah of Iran was' (here 'had' or 'was accompanied by' that band). The verb بود is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s597-g80: همه مرز is the subject phrase 'the whole frontier / all the borders'. در چنگ شیران بود is a locative + predicate 'was in the grip/clutches of the lions'; چنگ = 'claw/grip' and شیران here is used metaphorically as 'lions' = 'brave warriors'. The final بود is past 'was.'
segments.s597-g65: پس is an initial adverb meaning “then.” پشت او is a genitival/locative phrase literally “the rear/behind of him” (پشت + enclitic او). لشکر تازیان is a noun phrase “the army/host of the Arabs” (لشکر + postposed modifier تازیان). Together the phrase orders as: temporal adverb + locative ‘behind him’ + the identifying noun phrase.
segments.s597-g66: چو here is the comparative/simile “as/like.” منذرش is Munḏir with the enclitic -ش = “his Munḏir/his Munḏir (i.e. Munḏir belonging to him)” and functions as the head of the simile. یاور is a noun “ally/supporter” predicated of Munḏir; به سود و زیان is a prepositional phrase “in profit and loss / in good and ill” qualifying the scope of the alliance. Word order: simile marker + possessive name + predicate + adverbial phrase.
segments.s597-g67: اگر introduces a conditional “if.” خود is emphatic/reflexive “himself.” بگیرد is a present/subjunctive-form verb from گرفتن “to take/seize” (here: “should take” / hypothetical). سر گاه خویش is a compact phrase: سر + گاه = “chief place / high post / august time,” and خویش (خوی + enclitic ش) = “his own.” Together: “if he himself should take his own chief place.”
segments.s597-g68: به گیتی is a locative phrase “in the world” (به marking location/sphere). که introduces a relative/subordinate clause “that / so that.” باشد is the present/subjunctive form of بودن “to be” (here rendered as “would be / might be”). ز is the poetic comparative “than,” and بهرام بیش = “more than Bahram” (بیش ‘more’). Overall: “in the world, so that he would be more than Bahram.”
segments.s597-g69: ازان پس is a temporal adverb “after that / thereafter” (ازان = from that; پس = then/after). ز ایرانیانش literally “from/of his Iranians” (ز = from/with respect to; ایرانیان + enclitic ش = his Iranians). چه باک is a rhetorical question “what fear / why fear” (چه here exclamatory/interrogative and باک = fear/concern).
segments.s597-g70: This is a pair of rhetorical clauses: چه ما پیش او = “what are we before him?” (چه = interrog./exclam., ما = we, پیش = before/in the presence of, او = him). در چه یک مشت خاک = “(we are) in/amounting to what? a single handful of dust” where در introduces the predicative state and یک مشت خاک = “one handful of dust” (a diminutive image of insignificance). The repetition of چه frames both clauses as exclamatory/rhetorical.
segments.s597-g71: به بهرام گفتند is straightforward: به بهرام = “to Bahram,” گفتند = past 3rd-person plural “they said.” کای is a vocative particle “O.” فرمند here is a vocative form of address (a name or title); the vocative follows the reporting clause and introduces the direct speech.
segments.s597-g72: به شاهی marks the proposed office/role “to/for kingship” (به + شاهی = as king/for the kingship). توی جان ما is a locution “in our hearts” or “to our souls” (توی = in, جان ما = our soul/heart). را is the definite/object marker attached to ما, and پسند here denotes approval/pleasing — i.e. “you are pleasing/acceptable to our hearts” or “we approve you (for kingship).” Word order is somewhat inverted: goal (به شاهی) + locative (توی جان ما) + object marker + پسند (approval/predicate).
segments.s597-g17: جهان را is the definite/topic-marked object ('the world'); را marks it as the focused object. بدارم is a 1st-person subjunctive/optative of داشتن used here in the sense 'let me hold / let me keep' (literary). The phrases به رای و به داد are instrumental/causal prepositional phrases ('by counsel and by justice') with به + noun; word order places the object before the verb for emphasis, typical in classical Persian.
segments.s597-g18: چو here introduces a temporal/conditional clause ('when' / 'if'). ایمنی کنم is a causative construction with کنم ('I make/ensure safety'); کنم is present-subjunctive/optative used for the action 'to make secure'. باشم (subjunctive/optative of بودن) expresses the resulting state 'I shall be / I will be'. از with داد marks the cause/reason ('because of justice'), and شاد is a predicate adjective 'happy/glad'.
segments.s597-g19: کسی را که درویش باشد is a relative clause 'someone who is a dervish'; که introduces the relative clause and باشد is the subjunctive/present form of 'to be' used in classical register. را marks the person as the topical/definite target. به نیز follows to mean 'also / even to' (به = 'to/for', نیز = 'also'), placed after the clause for emphasis.
segments.s597-g20: ز (poetic form of از) means 'from'. گنج is 'treasure/treasury'. نهاده is a past participle 'placed/put' (here implying 'taken from / set aside in the treasury' modifying the thing given). ببخشیم is 1st-person-plural literary form 'we give / we bestow' (subjunctive/optative flavor in poetry). چیز is the indefinite noun 'a thing / something' — the direct object of ببخشیم.
segments.s597-g21: گنه کرده is a nominal phrase 'one who has committed a sin' (گنه + past participle کرده). را marks that person as the target/indirect object. پند پیش آوریم is a verbal phrase 'we bring/offer admonition' (پند = admonition; پیش آوریم = 'we bring forth/produce'), with آوریم 1st-person-plural present/optative of آوردن.
segments.s597-g22: چو introduces the conditional/temporal clause here ('if' / 'when'). دیگر کند means 'does again' (دیگر used adverbially = 'again / another time', کند = 'he does/makes'). بند پیش آوریم = 'we bring forth the bond/fetter' (بند = fetter/chain, پیش آوریم = 'we bring forward'), implying punishment if the sin is repeated.
segments.s597-g23: سپه را is the topical/definite object 'the army' (را). به هنگام is a temporal prepositional phrase 'at the time of / on the occasion of' (هنگام = 'at the time'). روزی here refers to sustenance/provisions (classical use 'daily provision'), but that specific sense is not present in the supplied lemma senses, so it is marked as not matched. دهیم is 1st-person-plural 'we give'. The whole phrase means 'we give the army its provisions at the appointed time.'
segments.s597-g24: خردمند را = 'the wise (one)' with را marking the person as the target. دلفروزی is a compound noun 'heart-delighting / cheerfulness' (دل + فروزی) meaning something that pleases the heart; دهیم is 'we give'. Together: 'to the wise we give delight / heart-pleasure.' Word order places the target before the thing given for emphasis.
segments.s597-g1: گذشت is a past-tense verb meaning “passed.” آن شب و بامداد پگاه is a time expression: آن شب (“that night”) coordinated with بامداد پگاه (“the dawn/early morning”); the two nouns are juxtaposed for poetic emphasis to mark the time when the next action occurs.
segments.s597-g2: بیامد and نشست are two past verbs placed in sequence: “he came” (بیامد) and “he sat” (نشست). از بر گاه شاه is a prepositional/location phrase: از بر here idiomatically indicates the king’s presence/seat (the court), with گاه = seat/place and شاه = king.
segments.s597-g3: فرستاد is a past verb “he sent/dispatched.” The conjunction و links it to بخواند, here a past/casual narrative verb meaning “called” or “summoned” (بخواند = ‘he called’); ایرانیان را uses را as the definite object marker on “the Iranians.” The two verbs present a linked sequence of actions.
segments.s597-g4: ز (poetic form of از) introduces a temporal phrase: ز روز گذشته = “from/since the previous day.” فراوان is an adverb “in great number/abundantly.” براند is a narrative verb meaning “he drove (away)” or “he drove off”; together the clause states that many were driven away since the day before.
segments.s597-g5: به آواز is an adverbial phrase meaning “in a voice” / “aloud” (به used adverbially). گفتند is third-person plural past “they said.” پس here means “then” and introduces موبدان (the mōbeds/priests) as the speakers.
segments.s597-g6: که introduces reported speech or a content clause: هستی تو is a poetic inversion of تو هستی (“you are”), with هستی functioning as the copula/verb “are.” داناتر is the comparative “more learned/wise,” and از marks the standard of comparison: داناتر از بخردان = “wiser than the sages.”
segments.s597-g7: به شاهنشهی در is a locative phrase “at/unto the imperial throne/court” (به ... در = ‘to/at’). چه is the interrogative “what,” and پیش آوری is a question of presenting/offering: آوری is second-person present/subjunctive/indicative “you bring” (poetic present used for future/intent), so چه پیش آوری = “what will you bring forward?”
segments.s597-g8: چو is a conditional/temporal conjunction “when/if.” گیری is the second-person singular active form “you take/seize.” بلندی و کنداوری are the two nouns in the conditional clause (بلندی = loftiness/high rank; کنداوری/Kنداوری is here a proper name or title). The clause sets up a condition: “If/when you seize/take loftiness and Kandaūrī...”
segments.s597-g81: گروهی is an indefinite plural subject meaning “some”/“a number (of people).” The preposition به marks the beneficiary/target (“for Bahram”), بهرام is the proper-name object, and باشند is the 3rd-person plural present copula (‘are’) linking to the predicate adjective شاد (“happy/glad”) that follows it.
segments.s597-g82: ز is a causal/prepositional marker here (“because of / from”), taking خسرو as its complement. دگر is an adverb “again/newly” placed before the verbal phrase; پاره + گیرند is a light/verbal phrase meaning ‘to rend/take to pieces’ with گیرند the 3rd-person plural present verb. یاد is the direct object (“memory/remembrance”) at the end of the clause.
segments.s597-g83: ز داد introduces the cause/ground (“by/through justice”). آن‌چنان ... که is the correlative construction “so ... that”; به here functions as a classical linking particle within that construction. پیمان تست is the result clause, where تست is an archaic copular/possessive form meaning ‘is (yours)’, not the modern English word “test.”
segments.s597-g84: ازان پس is a temporal phrase “from that / thereafter” (ازان = ‘from that’, پس = ‘after/then’). جهان is the subject “the world,” زیر فرمان is a prepositional phrase literally “under the command/authority of,” and تست again functions as the archaic copula/possessive ‘is yours’ closing the sentence.
segments.s597-g85: بهانه is the noun “pretext/excuse.” همان is a demonstrative here (“that/that very one”) modifying شیر “lion.” جنگیست is the copula + adjective meaning “is warlike” (جنگی + است contracted), and بس is a postpositive adverb “enough/that is all.”
segments.s597-g86: ازین پس is a temporal adverbial phrase “from this time on / henceforth” (از + این). بزرگی is the thing sought (“greatness”), نجویند is the present plural stem of جستن/جَویدن with the negative prefix ن- here meaning “do not seek” or “will not seek,” and کس is the indefinite postverbal noun used here in the negative sense “anyone / no one.”
segments.s597-g87: بدان is a fused classical locative/target phrase (ب- + دان) meaning “to that / with regard to that.” گشت is the past of گشتن ‘became/turned (into),’ so the clause reads ‘(to) that became Bahram like‑minded’ — i.e., ‘Bahram thus became like‑minded.’ همداستان is the predicate adjective ‘like‑minded / of one mind.’
segments.s597-g88: که introduces a subordinate/relative clause “which/that.” آورد is a past-tense verb here meaning ‘related/recounted’ (bring/relate), او is the subject “he,” and پیش ازین (پیش + از + ین) is the adverbial “before this / previously.” داستان is the object “story/tale”; word order (verb before subject and the time-phrase) is typical of poetic/classical Persian.
segments.s597-g89: چنین بود آیین شاهان داد is a compact clause: چنین (thus) + بود (past copula 'was') introduces a general statement; آیین شاهان داد is a noun phrase where آیین ('custom; practice') is qualified by شاهان داد — here شاهان (kings) with داد functioning as an adjectival qualifier 'just' (i.e. 'kings of justice') so the whole yields 'the custom/practice of the just kings.' Word order is typical Persian: adverb + copula + noun phrase.
segments.s597-g90: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'so that'); چون here functions temporally ('when' / 'as soon as') rather than its literal 'like/as' sense; نو ('new') modifies the following noun; بدی in this context is an archaic/potential variant not matching the listed nominal senses (likely an old verbal form meaning 'was made / came to be'), so its sense is uncertain; شاه فرخ‌نژاد is the noun phrase 'the king Farrokh-nēzhād.' The clause therefore reads 'that when a new king Farrokh-nēzhād ...' and syntactically sets up the events that follow.
segments.s597-g91: بر او is a prepositional phrase 'upon him' (بر + pronoun او). شدی is an older/past occurrence verb here ('happened' / 'came') used impersonally or intransitively; موبد موبدان is an appositional title 'mōbed of mōbeds' (the chief priest). Word order places the circumstance (بر او) before the verb and the title after it, typical of older Persian narrative.
segments.s597-g92: ببردی is a past-tense verbal form meaning 'took' / 'carried off' (an older inflection; here third-person past). سه بینادل is the direct object 'three [بینادل]' — بینادل is a rare/uncertain word and likely a technical or corrupt reading (left untranslated as a lexical item). از بخردان is a prepositional phrase 'from the wise (men)' where بخردان is the plural/collective of 'wise.' The verb takes the object before the source phrase, as usual.
segments.s597-g93: همو شاه is a topicalized subject 'that same king' (همو = 'the same one'); بر گاه is a locative phrase 'on the throne/at the seat' (بر + گاه), and بنشاندی is a past transitive verb 'they seated (him)' or 'he was seated' — an older past form of نشاندن. The order (subject — locative — verb) is narrative and typical of the epic register.
segments.s597-g94: بدان here is the composite sense 'به + آن' ('to/at that'), introducing the object 'تاج' (crown). بر آفرین appears as the name or epithet attached to the crown (آفرین in the sense of an acclamation or name), and خواندی is a past verb 'they called' / 'they named' (an older past form of خواندن used in the sense 'to name/call'). The whole phrase means 'they called that crown "Bar-Afarin."'
segments.s597-g95: نهادی here is a past verbal form from نهادن ('to place, to set') used in the coronation sense 'they put / placed'; the provided noun senses do not fit well, so the exact listed sense is uncertain. به نام کیان is 'the name/title "Kian"' preceded by the dative/benefactive به; بر سرش ('on his head') consists of the locative preposition بر + سر ('head') plus the enclitic ش ('his'). Grammatically this is a transitive construction 'they put [X] on his head.'
segments.s597-g96: بسودی is an older verbal form meaning roughly 'they bestowed' / 'it availed' (the listed nominal senses are not a perfect fit, so sense is uncertain here). به شادی is an adverbial phrase 'with joy / in celebration.' دو رخ is a numeric + noun 'two rukh' (رخ usually 'face/appearance' here used as a ceremonial item or epithet; the exact ceremonial sense is idiomatic). بر برش repeats a locative/benefactive pattern: بر + برش (here the final بر is a preposition and برش likely an enclitic form meaning 'on him' or a construction marking the target). Overall the line describes conferring two rukh upon the new king amid rejoicing. Several of these words are archaic or specialized ceremonial terms, so their precise modern gloss is uncertain.
segments.s597-g41: 'برین' is a prepositional phrase 'بر + این' (= 'upon this'); poetic orthography omits an overt ezāfe. 'پاک یزدان' (pure God) is the object of that preposition. 'گوای' is a noun 'witness' used predicatively, and 'منست' is an archaic predicative/copular form meaning roughly 'is to me' or 'is fitting/appropriate' — here functioning like 'is my' / 'is my witness'. Word order is emphatic: prepositional phrase first, then the predicate.
segments.s597-g42: The clause begins with the noun 'خرد' ('wisdom') as the topical subject. 'بر زبان' = 'upon the tongue' (a locative/metaphorical phrase: 'in speech'). 'رهنمای' is a noun 'guide' and again 'منست' serves as the archaic predicative/copula ('is fitting/serves as'). Together: 'Wisdom, upon the tongue, is (my) guide.'
segments.s597-g43: 'همان' is emphatic 'that / the very same'. The phrase lists two titles joined by 'و': the simple 'موبد' (mōbed, Zoroastrian priest) and the compound/title 'موبد موبدان' (literally 'mōbed [of] mōbdān' — i.e. the chief or mōbed of the mōbedān). In epic poetry the ezāfe or explicit connector between the two nouns is often compressed.
segments.s597-g44: 'پسندیده' and 'کاردیده' are adjectival epithets: 'پسندیده' = 'chosen/approved' and 'کاردیده' = 'experienced / time‑tested'. 'ردان' here is an archaic/poetic participial noun (from رَدّ/رَدن) meaning 'those who reject/repel' or 'rejecters' and functions as a final epithet attached to the priests. The sequence lists qualities: approved, experienced, (the) rejecting ones.
segments.s597-g45: 'برین کار' = 'on/over this affair' (prepositional phrase + demonstrative). 'یک سال‌گر' is a personified/time phrase ('one-year bringer' — i.e. a year) used conditionally, and 'بگذرد' is the present/subjunctive form 'may pass / should pass' (classical poetic usage). Together: 'If a year passes in this affair...'.
segments.s597-g46: 'نپیچم' is negative + present/subjunctive of پیچیدن ('not turn / not deviate'); here it means 'I will not turn away / I will not swerve'. 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from'. 'گفتار جان و خرد' = 'the utterance/speech of (my) soul and of wisdom' — i.e. the counsel coming from soul and wisdom. The whole clause means 'I will not depart from the counsel of soul and wisdom.'
segments.s597-g47: 'ز' = 'from/of' (poetic). 'میراث' = 'inheritance' and 'بیزارم' is the first‑person predicate 'I am averse/loath to' (here 'I loathe / am weary of'). The phrase continues 'و تاج و تخت' = 'and crown and throne' — a list of worldly titles/possessions the speaker rejects.
segments.s597-g48: 'ازان پس' = 'after that / thereafter'. 'نشینم' is the first‑person present/subjunctive of 'نشستن' ('I will sit' / 'I shall sit'). 'بر شوربخت' = 'upon the unfortunate' (literally 'on/over the ill‑fated'), meaning the speaker will take a seat among or adopt the lot of the unlucky/unfortunate. The clause expresses renunciation followed by embracing misfortune.
segments.s597-g25: همان راست = a noun phrase 'that same/right straightness' (همان as a determiner 'the same'); داریم is the present plural of داشتن 'we hold/maintain'; دل با زبان is the phrase 'heart with tongue' (دل = object/what is kept straight; با زبان = instrument 'by/with the tongue'). The word order places the maintained quality (همان راست) before the verb دارم and the means (با زبان) after it.
segments.s597-g26: ز کژی و تاری = a prepositional phrase 'from/by crookedness and darkness' (ز = 'from/of' in poetic register); بپیچم is ب + پیچم, present/subjunctive/optative first-person singular 'I turn/turn aside' (the ب- marks present/subjunctive in classical Persian); روان is the object 'mind/soul' receiving the action. Together the clause means 'I turn the mind away from crookedness and gloom.'
segments.s597-g27: کسی کو بمیرد = an archaic relative clause 'someone who dies' (کسی = 'someone'; کو is the older relative particle equivalent to modern که); بمیرد is the present/subjunctive/relative form 'dies'. نباشدش = negative existential/possessive construction 'is not for him / he does not have'; خویش (with the enclitic ش = his) means 'his own' (here indicating what belongs to him). The whole segment states that when someone dies, his own (things) are not any longer his.
segments.s597-g28: وزو چیز ماند ز اندازه بیش = 'and something remains of him beyond measure.' وزو is an archaic contraction (و ز او = 'and of him/from him'); چیز ماند = 'a thing remains' (ماند present/past-stem meaning 'remains, is left'); ز اندازه بیش = a prepositional phrase 'beyond measure' (ز = 'from/of', اندازه = 'measure/degree', بیش = 'excess/more'). The clause says that something about him outlasts measure.
segments.s597-g29: به دوریش = 'because of his/her/its being far' (به + دوری + -ش possessive); بخشم here is the verb 'I give / I bestow' (present first-person); نیارم is negative/contrastive in form (in context 'I will not bring/put'); به گنج = 'into/to the treasury/treasure' (به as directional). Taken together: 'On account of his distance, I will not bring/give (my) gifts into the treasury.'
segments.s597-g30: نبندم = negative form (ن + ببندم) meaning 'I will not bind/close/lock' (first-person singular subjunctive/optative form used for a negated resolve); دل = 'heart' (direct object); اندر = 'in/within' (archaic preposition); سرای سپنج = 'the house/room of sponge' (سرای = 'house/place', سپنج = 'sponge'), a metaphorical place. The clause literally says 'I will not lock my heart inside a sponge-house.'
segments.s597-g31: همه رای = 'every/ all counsel/plan' (همه = all; رای = counsel/plan); با کاردانان = 'with the experts' (با = with; کاردانان = plural of کاردان 'skilled/knowledgeable people'); زنیم is the present first-person plural of زدن, used idiomatically with رای (رای زدن) meaning 'to deliberate/compose a plan' or 'to lay down counsel' — so the clause means 'we form all counsel together with the experts.'
segments.s597-g32: به تدبیر = 'by/with prudence' (به + تدبیر as instrumental); پشت هوا = 'the back of desire' (پشت = 'back/rear', هوا = 'desire/passion/whim'); بشکنیم is the present/subjunctive first-person plural 'we break' (ب + شکنیم), often used as a resolve or general present. The clause is metaphorical: 'with counsel/foresight we break the back of desire.'
segments.s597-g97: ازان پس = a temporal phrase 'from that (time) on, thereafter' (ازان = 'from that', پس = 'after/then'). هرانکس که is a free relative 'whoever / whosoever' introducing a clause. بردی is the past form (2nd person) of بردن 'to take/bring' used here in epic address ('you brought/took'); نثار is a noun 'offering, gift' and is the object of بردی. The clause therefore means 'whoever brought an offering' and sets up what follows.
segments.s597-g98: به خواهنده = prepositional phrase 'to/for the petitioner/requester' (خواهنده = 'one who asks/requests'). دادی is the 2nd-person past tense 'you gave'; همی is a habitual/adverbial particle 'always/ever' used here to mark repeated action; شهریار is placed after the verb as a vocative or direct address 'O king' (epic style often appends the addressee after the verb). Together: 'you would always give it to the petitioner, O king.'
segments.s597-g99: به موبد سپردند is a past-tense clause 'they entrusted to the mobed' (به + recipient, سپردند = 3rd-person plural past of سپردن 'to entrust/hand over'). پس = 'then' (linking to the next action). تاج و تخت = 'the crown and the throne' (direct objects/nominal phrase). Word order here is typical epic inversion: prepositional phrase + verb + then the objects.
segments.s597-g100: به هامون شد is an intransitive-use of شد 'became/was moved' with به + place 'to the Hamun/plain' (i.e. 'it came to the plain'). از شهر introduces origin 'from the city ...' and بیداربخت is an epithet or name attached to شهر ('the ill‑fated city' / possibly a place-name). So the clause expresses motion/origin: 'they/it went to the Hamun from the city Bīdārbakht.'
segments.s597-g101: دو شیر ژیان = 'two living lions' (دو = two; شیر = lion; ژیان used adjectivally 'living'). داشت is the past 'had'. گستهم is a proper name (here the possessor) placed after the verb — a common epic inversion (V S order). گرد here is an adverb 'about/around' or a poetic residue linked to motion/context; the whole phrase functions as 'Gostaham had two living lions (about/about him).' The clause therefore establishes possession.
segments.s597-g102: به زنجیر بسته is a participial phrase: بسته = past participle 'bound/tied' and به زنجیر = 'with a chain/onto a chain' so 'having bound [them] with chains'. به موبد سپرد = 'he entrusted to the mobed' (سپرد here 3rd-person past 'entrusted/handed over'). The omitted direct object (the lions) is understood from the previous clause; the sequence shows an action performed on them then handed over.
segments.s597-g103: ببردند is a past 3rd‑plural form 'they carried/took away' (epic/archaic prefix ب- with بردند). شیران جنگی = 'war‑lions' (شیران = lions; جنگی = warlike / martial) where جنگی qualifies شیران. کشان is a present/adjectival element 'dragging, pulling' (‑کشان used to indicate the manner). Together: 'they carried off the war‑lions, dragging them.'
segments.s597-g104: کشنده شد = 'became deadly/killing' (کشنده used predicatively + شد 'became'). از بیم = 'out of/from fear' (از = from; بیم = fear). چون = conjunction 'as/so that' introducing a comparison or consequence. بیهشان is an archaic formation: بیه 'without' + شان (their) => 'as if without (them) / bereft' — here used poetically to convey stupefaction or helplessness. Grammatically this clause gives the result or effect: 'they became deadly; out of fear they were as if bereft.'

sentences.s597-s53: They fastened the ivory throne to its pedestal and set the crown upon a corner of the ivory.
sentences.s597-s54: The whole world watched that crown and throne to see how the affairs of that fortunate one fared.
sentences.s597-s55: If the king becomes victorious in this, then the nobles will acclaim him.
sentences.s597-s5: What can you bring forward from justice and from righteousness by which crookedness and deficiency will be lost?
sentences.s597-s6: Thus he gave an answer to the sages, to those renowned and to the brave.
sentences.s597-s7: That I may increase generosity — I will refrain from disputation, from injustice and from seeking (vain pursuit).
sentences.s597-s8: Where is anyone who deserves kingship? I will rightly bestow the land upon them.
sentences.s597-s17: I ask counsel and speak plainly; when someone undertakes a new action, I will root it out.
sentences.s597-s18: I will not seek to break up the assembly for anyone who asks justice of me.
sentences.s597-s19: I give justice to the one who asked for it; I will not direct my speech toward anything except the truth.
sentences.s597-s20: I will exact punishment on him with evil, in such a way as is fitting by the ways of kings.
sentences.s597-s29: God made him out of Justice; may nothing bad befall him.
sentences.s597-s30: If by speech we can endure at all, we will put Wisdom’s head to sleep.
sentences.s597-s31: We shall find all good things from him, and place upon his face food and justice.
sentences.s597-s32: In this high pedestal and with this horn and mane, in the world there is no one who is his equal.
sentences.s597-s25: When those wise ones—the great men and the wakeful‑hearted mōbeds—heard the reply.
sentences.s597-s26: Because of what had been said they became regretful; the guilty turned toward a remedy.
sentences.s597-s27: They said aloud to one another that he was more deserving of the kingship (the throne).
sentences.s597-s28: In manliness, speech, counsel, and lineage, no one in the world was born purer.
sentences.s597-s37: No one knew, among your arts, of the purity of your body, your learning, and your counsel.
sentences.s597-s38: When Khosrow, who was of an earlier lineage, became king, they cried 'Afarin.'
sentences.s597-s39: We are all under oath and bond, for he says that we are in harm.
sentences.s597-s40: From then on the Shah of Iran had a band; the whole frontier was in the claws of the lions (i.e. in the grip of brave men).
sentences.s597-s33: Then behind him stood the Arab host, like Munḏir his ally in both gain and loss.
sentences.s597-s34: If he himself should seize his own high place in the world so that he would be greater than Bahram.
sentences.s597-s35: After that, what fear from his Iranians? What are we before him but a single handful of dust.
sentences.s597-s36: They said to Bahram, “O Formand, accept kingship — you are pleasing to our hearts.”
sentences.s597-s9: Let me hold the world by counsel and by justice; when I make security, I shall be glad because of justice.
sentences.s597-s10: We bestow something from the treasure even upon one who is a dervish.
sentences.s597-s11: We bring admonition before one who has sinned; if he does it again, we bring forth the fetters.
sentences.s597-s12: We give the army its provisions at the appointed time; to the wise we give heart-delight.
sentences.s597-s1: That night passed, and at dawn he came and sat in the king’s presence.
sentences.s597-s2: He sent (men) and summoned the Iranians; from the previous day he had driven many away.
sentences.s597-s3: Then the mōbeds said aloud: “Who are you? You are wiser than the sages.”
sentences.s597-s4: ‘What will you present before the imperial throne?’ — ‘If you seize high rank and Kandaūrī...’
sentences.s597-s41: Some are glad for Bahram; others, because of Khosrow, again tear the memory to pieces.
sentences.s597-s42: By such justice—so great that the covenant is yours—after that the world is under your command.
sentences.s597-s43: The pretext is that very warlike lion, and that is enough; from now on no one seeks greatness.
sentences.s597-s44: Thus Bahram became of the same mind, for he had related that story before.
sentences.s597-s45: Such was the custom of the just kings, that when a new king, Farrokh-nēzhād, ...
sentences.s597-s46: Upon him the chief of the mōbeds came, and he took three bīnadels from the wise men.
sentences.s597-s47: That same king they seated on the throne; they named that crown 'Bar-Afarin.'
sentences.s597-s48: They set upon his head the name 'Kian'; with rejoicing they bestowed two rukh upon him.
sentences.s597-s21: Upon this pure God is my witness; wisdom upon the tongue is my guide.
sentences.s597-s22: That very mōbed — and the mōbed of mōbeds — approved and well‑tried, the rejecters.
sentences.s597-s23: If, in this matter, a year should pass, I will not turn away from the sayings of soul and wisdom.
sentences.s597-s24: I am alienated from inheritance, and from crown and throne; after that I will sit upon the unfortunate.
sentences.s597-s13: We keep the same straightness in our heart, and with our tongue I turn the mind away from crookedness and darkness.
sentences.s597-s14: When a man dies he no longer has his own (things), and something of him remains beyond measure.
sentences.s597-s15: Because of his distance I will not place my gift in the treasury; I will not lock my heart within a sponge-house.
sentences.s597-s16: We set every plan with the skilled; with prudence we will break the back of desire.
sentences.s597-s49: From then on, O king, whatever anyone offered as a gift, you would always give to the petitioner.
sentences.s597-s50: They entrusted the crown and throne to the mobed; then they were taken to the plain from the ill‑fated city.
sentences.s597-s51: Gostaham had two living lions; having bound them with chains, he entrusted them to the mobed.
sentences.s597-s52: They carried off the war‑lions, dragging them; they became deadly — from fear they were as if bereft.
